beats.text: You read and created bar graphs with scale of 10. You figured out values at the lines and between the lines. Let's look at some patterns you discovered.

incorrects.description: Student clicked a wrong graph on first attempt

steps.text: Not quite. Count the lines on each graph. Which one has the fewest?

incorrects.description: Student clicked a wrong graph on second attempt

steps.text: Let's count together. Scale of 1 has 20 lines. Scale of 2 has 10 lines. Scale of 5 has 4 lines. Scale of 10 has just 2 lines. Which has the fewest?

incorrects.description: Student clicked a wrong graph three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: Scale of 10 graph highlights to show correct answer.

steps.text: Let me show you. The graph with scale of 10 has only 2 lines to reach 20. That's fewer than any other scale. When each line counts for more, you need fewer lines. Click the scale of 10 graph.

incorrects.description: Student clicked a wrong category on first attempt

steps.text: Not quite. Look at where the bar ends on the axis. Which category's bar lands between 30 and 40?

incorrects.description: Student clicked a wrong category on second attempt

params.description: Category A bar highlights in vertical graph.

steps.text: Look at Category A. The bar ends between the 30 and 40 gridlines. That means it shows 35. Which category is that?

incorrects.description: Student clicked a wrong category three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: Category A bar in vertical graph highlights and confirms.

steps.text: Let me show you. I look at the axis and find where each bar ends. Category A ends between 30 and 40, halfway between those gridlines. That position means 35. The answer is Category A.

incorrects.description: Student clicked a wrong category on first attempt

steps.text: Almost. Find the bar that ends between 30 and 40 on the horizontal axis.

incorrects.description: Student clicked a wrong category on second attempt

params.description: Category A bar highlights in horizontal graph.

steps.text: Look at Category A in the horizontal graph. The bar ends between 30 and 40, just like in the vertical graph. Which category shows 35?

incorrects.description: Student clicked a wrong category three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: Category A bar in horizontal graph highlights and confirms.

steps.text: I'll walk you through it. The axis goes from 0 to 50 in both graphs. In the horizontal graph, Category A's bar ends between 30 and 40, halfway between those lines. That position is 35, the same as in the vertical graph. Click Category A.

incorrects.description: Branch 2: Student selected only A and B (correct answers, but checking completeness)

steps.text: You selected situations that use 10s. Look at all three images again. Are there any others that fit?

incorrects.description: Branch 3: Student selected only C or nothing (no correct answers)

steps.text: Think about where you count by 10s in real life. Money comes in tens. Rulers have marks at 10, 20, 30. Which images show that?

incorrects.description: Branch 4: Student selected C plus one or both correct answers (mixed correct and incorrect)

steps.text: You found situations with 10s. But counting one by one is not using a scale of 10. Look at C again. Does it show groups of 10?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Image A highlights and pulses to confirm.
Image B highlights and pulses to confirm.

steps.text: Let me show you. Image A shows money. Bills come in tens, so we count by 10s. Image B shows a ruler. Rulers have marks at 10, 20, 30. Both use a scale of 10. Image C counts one by one, not by 10s. The answers are A and B.

condition.selected: I picked the line closest to the top of the bar

incorrects.description: Student selected the closest-line strategy, which gives approximate rather than precise values

params.description: Ghost gridlines at 5s pulse to show halfway marks between labeled lines

steps.text: Picking the closest line helps when you estimate, but this bar ends exactly halfway between 40 and 50. What's halfway between those two numbers?

condition.selected: I added the two numbers on the lines

incorrects.description: Student selected adding the gridline numbers, which doesn't work for finding values between lines

params.description: Ghost gridlines at 5s pulse. The bar top aligns with the halfway gridline between 40 and 50

steps.text: Adding 40 and 50 gives 90, not 45. The bar sits halfway between 40 and 50. What number is halfway between them?

incorrects.description: Student selected guessing, missing the systematic halfway strategy

params.description: Ghost gridlines at 5s pulse to highlight the halfway pattern

steps.text: You don't need to guess. The bar sits exactly halfway between 40 and 50. The scale is 10, so each gap splits into 5 and 5. What's 40 plus 5?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: The halfway gridline at 45 pulses, then a visual shows 40 + 5 = 45

steps.text: Let me show you the pattern. The scale is 10, so lines go 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50. Halfway between any two lines is 5 more than the lower one. Between 40 and 50, halfway is 45. That's 40 plus 5. The bar ends right at that halfway point, so the value is 45. Finding halfway between lines is the strategy that works every time with scale of 10.
